title: Melanoma
oneLiner: Malignant skin neoplasm from melanocytes; high metastatic potential; early detection and excision are life-saving.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Metastatic cerebral oedema / herniation** – if patient presents with headache, vomiting, new focal neurology, or GCS drop → single finding: *papilloedema or Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia + hypertension)* → urgent IV dexamethasone 10mg stat + CT head / neurosurgery review
- **Severe immune-related adverse event (irAE)** in melanoma patient on immunotherapy (e.g., nivolumab/pembrolizumab) → single finding: *hypotension + hypoxia or frank haemorrhagic colitis* → must stop immunotherapy, check cortisol/serum lactate, contact oncology immediately
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - New or changing pigmented lesion (ABCDE: Asymmetry, Border irregular, Colour variegation, Diameter >6mm, Evolution)
  - Itching, bleeding, ulceration, or crusting in a stable mole
  - Amelanotic nodules (raised pink/red lesions) – often missed
  - Lymphadenopathy (especially in-transit metastases)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Full skin check (including scalp, soles, nails, genitalia)
  - Lymph node exam (axillae, groins, neck) – fixed/matted nodes suggest advanced disease
  - Dermoscopy: pigmented network (in benign) vs. atypical network, blue-white veil, regression structures
  - Palpation for satellite nodules within 2cm of primary lesion
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Seborrhoeic keratosis** – stuck-on appearance, horn cysts, no change
- **Atypical naevus** – well-defined borders, symmetrical, stable
- **Pigmented basal cell carcinoma** – rolled edges, telangiectasia, pearly border
- **Dermatofibroma** – dimple sign on lateral compression, firm, brownish
- **Blue naevus** – uniform dark blue, lifelong, no change
- **Pyogenic granuloma** – rapid growth, friable, bleeds easily, not pigmented
- **Malignant blue naevus / Spitzoid tumour** – rare, confirmed on histology
- **Splitter:** *Dermoscopy showing regression structures (fibrosis, peppering) strongly suggests melanoma over atypical naevus; if in doubt, excise.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Dermoscopy (handheld dermatoscope) – perform in all suspicious lesions; record photographs
  - Lymph node palpation
- **Bloods:**
  - Full blood count (normocytic anaemia if advanced)
  - LDH (elevated = poor prognosis, stage IV risk)
  - Liver function tests (metastases)
  - Baseline autoimmune screen if starting immunotherapy (TSH, cortisol, LFTs, CRP)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: Wide local excision with histology (diagnostic gold standard)
  - For staging: CT neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis if stage ≥IIB (or any with palpable nodes)
  - MRI brain if neurological symptoms or high-risk stage (e.g., stage III/IV)
  - PET-CT if metastasis suspected but not confirmed on CT
- **Special tests:**
  - Punch or incisional biopsy (avoid shave excision if possible – cannot assess Breslow thickness)
  - Immunohistochemistry: S100, HMB-45, Melan-A
  - Molecular testing: BRAF V600 mutation (for targeted therapy – dabrafenib/trametinib)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Breslow thickness (mm) – strongest predictor of prognosis; measured from granular layer to deepest tumour cell
  - Clark level (I–V) – less reproducible; used historically
  - AJCC staging (8th edition): definitive after excision + sentinel node biopsy
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Full-thickness excisional biopsy (with 2mm margin)** – shave biopsy or curettage will corrupt Breslow measurement and prevent accurate staging
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected melanoma: **do not incise, do not cauterise** – refer to dermatology/dermatopathology for excision
  - If septic from infected ulcerated lesion: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin + metronidazole) after cultures
  - If neurological symptoms: IV dexamethasone 10mg stat + CT head + neurosurgery
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm histology – Breslow, margins, ulceration
  - Arrange sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) if Breslow ≥0.8mm or <0.8mm with adverse features (e.g., ulceration)
  - Staging imaging (CT if stage ≥IIB)
  - Immunotherapy referral – typically 3–6 cycles if metastatic (e.g., nivolumab/ipilimumab)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Wide local excision (WLE) with 1–2cm margin (depending on Breslow)
  - Follow-up: clinical exam every 3 months for 3 years, then 6–12 monthly (NICE)
  - Sun protection counselling; family screening for first-degree relatives
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Maintenance IV fluids (e.g., 0.9% saline 1 litre/8h) if NBM for SLNB
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if no bleeding risk)
  - Diet: NBM from midnight if surgery next day; otherwise full diet
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2; 4-hourly if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Wide local excision – no drug required
  - SLNB – no drug (sentinel node mapping with patent blue dye + radioisotope)
  - If stage IV (BRAF wild-type): pembrolizumab 200mg IV q3w or nivolumab 240mg IV q2w (per BNF)
  - If BRAF V600 mutant: dabrafenib 150mg PO BD + trametinib 2mg PO OD (per BNF)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ipilimumab 3mg/kg IV q3w (4 doses) – may be used in combination with nivolumab
  - Vemurafenib (BRAF inhibitor) + cobimetinib
  - Interferon-alpha – rarely used now due to toxicity
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Immunotherapy contraindicated in active autoimmune disease (unless controlled, e.g., hypothyroidism on replacement)
  - BRAF inhibitors can cause photosensitivity (use sunscreen)
  - Trametinib – avoid with strong CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampicin)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Immunotherapy doses generally not adjusted for mild–moderate renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic impairment – [check local policy]
  - Dabrafenib/trametinib – no adjustment for renal; reduce dose if GFR <30 (consult BNF)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Immunotherapy + steroids** – combining with high-dose steroids (prednisolone >10mg/day) may reduce antitumour efficacy; only use for life-threatening irAEs.
  - **BRAF/MEK inhibitors** – starting in asymptomatic brain metastases without local treatment (radiosurgery) can cause intratumoural haemorrhage.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Immune-related colitis (diarrhoea ± blood) – check stool cultures, stop immunotherapy, IV methylprednisolone if grade ≥3
  - Immune-related pneumonitis – new cough/dyspnoea → high-res CT, hold immunotherapy
  - Anaphylaxis to immune checkpoint inhibitors (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Lymphoedema after SLNB or dissection (early physiotherapy)
  - Tumour recurrence – in-transit metastases, locoregional
  - Secondary cancers (risk higher with BRAF inhibitors – cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, keratoacanthoma)
sections.Prognosis: - **5-year survival:** Stage I ~98%; stage II ~85–95%; stage III ~40–80% (depending on nodal burden); stage IV ~20% (with immunotherapy, 5-year survival up to 50% in some trials). Prognosis heavily driven by Breslow thickness, ulceration, and mitotic rate.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always dermoscope any pigmented lesion** – even if you think it’s benign; it only takes 30 seconds.
- **Take a clinical photograph** before biopsy – essential for surgical planning and medicolegal record.
- **Check BRAF status on all stage III/IV melanomas** – opens targeted therapy options.
- **In immunotherapy patients, check a baseline cortisol and TSH before each cycle** – adrenal insufficiency can present insidiously.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **NEVER shave biopsy a suspicious pigmented lesion** – it destroys Breslow measurement.
- **Do not excise a melanoma with narrow margins (<1cm)** – recurrence risk rises.
- **Do not automatically start steroids for immunotherapy side effects without consulting oncology** – they may blunt therapeutic response.
- **Missing in-transit metastases** – they are palpable nodules <2cm from primary scar; excise with clear margin.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New or changing pigmented lesion elsewhere
  - Unexplained fever, rash, diarrhoea, or new cough (suspected irAE)
  - Swollen lymph nodes (especially axilla/groin)
  - Headache lasting >48 hours or any neurological symptom
- **Follow-up:**
  - Dermatology review in 2 weeks for excision results
  - Oncology referral if stage ≥IIB or high-risk features
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (SpR/Consultant) if:**
  - Suspicious lesion in a pregnant woman (melanoma can metastasise to placenta)
  - Rapidly growing melanotic lesion with satellite nodules (indicates aggressive disease)
  - Immunotherapy patient with any of: temperature >38°C, new confusion, hypotension, hypoxia, haemoptysis, or bloody diarrhoea >6 stools/day
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - GCS ≤12
  - Hypoxia (SpO₂ <90% on room air) – think pneumonitis
  - Septic shock (MAP <65 despite fluids)

rawMarkdown:
## Melanoma
**One-liner:** Malignant skin neoplasm from melanocytes; high metastatic potential; early detection and excision are life-saving.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Metastatic cerebral oedema / herniation** – if patient presents with headache, vomiting, new focal neurology, or GCS drop → single finding: *papilloedema or Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia + hypertension)* → urgent IV dexamethasone 10mg stat + CT head / neurosurgery review
- **Severe immune-related adverse event (irAE)** in melanoma patient on immunotherapy (e.g., nivolumab/pembrolizumab) → single finding: *hypotension + hypoxia or frank haemorrhagic colitis* → must stop immunotherapy, check cortisol/serum lactate, contact oncology immediately

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - New or changing pigmented lesion (ABCDE: Asymmetry, Border irregular, Colour variegation, Diameter >6mm, Evolution)
  - Itching, bleeding, ulceration, or crusting in a stable mole
  - Amelanotic nodules (raised pink/red lesions) – often missed
  - Lymphadenopathy (especially in-transit metastases)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Full skin check (including scalp, soles, nails, genitalia)
  - Lymph node exam (axillae, groins, neck) – fixed/matted nodes suggest advanced disease
  - Dermoscopy: pigmented network (in benign) vs. atypical network, blue-white veil, regression structures
  - Palpation for satellite nodules within 2cm of primary lesion

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Seborrhoeic keratosis** – stuck-on appearance, horn cysts, no change
- **Atypical naevus** – well-defined borders, symmetrical, stable
- **Pigmented basal cell carcinoma** – rolled edges, telangiectasia, pearly border
- **Dermatofibroma** – dimple sign on lateral compression, firm, brownish
- **Blue naevus** – uniform dark blue, lifelong, no change
- **Pyogenic granuloma** – rapid growth, friable, bleeds easily, not pigmented
- **Malignant blue naevus / Spitzoid tumour** – rare, confirmed on histology
- **Splitter:** *Dermoscopy showing regression structures (fibrosis, peppering) strongly suggests melanoma over atypical naevus; if in doubt, excise.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Dermoscopy (handheld dermatoscope) – perform in all suspicious lesions; record photographs
  - Lymph node palpation
- **Bloods:**
  - Full blood count (normocytic anaemia if advanced)
  - LDH (elevated = poor prognosis, stage IV risk)
  - Liver function tests (metastases)
  - Baseline autoimmune screen if starting immunotherapy (TSH, cortisol, LFTs, CRP)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: Wide local excision with histology (diagnostic gold standard)
  - For staging: CT neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis if stage ≥IIB (or any with palpable nodes)
  - MRI brain if neurological symptoms or high-risk stage (e.g., stage III/IV)
  - PET-CT if metastasis suspected but not confirmed on CT
- **Special tests:**
  - Punch or incisional biopsy (avoid shave excision if possible – cannot assess Breslow thickness)
  - Immunohistochemistry: S100, HMB-45, Melan-A
  - Molecular testing: BRAF V600 mutation (for targeted therapy – dabrafenib/trametinib)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Breslow thickness (mm) – strongest predictor of prognosis; measured from granular layer to deepest tumour cell
  - Clark level (I–V) – less reproducible; used historically
  - AJCC staging (8th edition): definitive after excision + sentinel node biopsy
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Full-thickness excisional biopsy (with 2mm margin)** – shave biopsy or curettage will corrupt Breslow measurement and prevent accurate staging

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected melanoma: **do not incise, do not cauterise** – refer to dermatology/dermatopathology for excision
  - If septic from infected ulcerated lesion: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin + metronidazole) after cultures
  - If neurological symptoms: IV dexamethasone 10mg stat + CT head + neurosurgery
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm histology – Breslow, margins, ulceration
  - Arrange sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) if Breslow ≥0.8mm or <0.8mm with adverse features (e.g., ulceration)
  - Staging imaging (CT if stage ≥IIB)
  - Immunotherapy referral – typically 3–6 cycles if metastatic (e.g., nivolumab/ipilimumab)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Wide local excision (WLE) with 1–2cm margin (depending on Breslow)
  - Follow-up: clinical exam every 3 months for 3 years, then 6–12 monthly (NICE)
  - Sun protection counselling; family screening for first-degree relatives
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Maintenance IV fluids (e.g., 0.9% saline 1 litre/8h) if NBM for SLNB
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if no bleeding risk)
  - Diet: NBM from midnight if surgery next day; otherwise full diet
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2; 4-hourly if stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Wide local excision – no drug required
  - SLNB – no drug (sentinel node mapping with patent blue dye + radioisotope)
  - If stage IV (BRAF wild-type): pembrolizumab 200mg IV q3w or nivolumab 240mg IV q2w (per BNF)
  - If BRAF V600 mutant: dabrafenib 150mg PO BD + trametinib 2mg PO OD (per BNF)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ipilimumab 3mg/kg IV q3w (4 doses) – may be used in combination with nivolumab
  - Vemurafenib (BRAF inhibitor) + cobimetinib
  - Interferon-alpha – rarely used now due to toxicity
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Immunotherapy contraindicated in active autoimmune disease (unless controlled, e.g., hypothyroidism on replacement)
  - BRAF inhibitors can cause photosensitivity (use sunscreen)
  - Trametinib – avoid with strong CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampicin)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Immunotherapy doses generally not adjusted for mild–moderate renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic impairment – [check local policy]
  - Dabrafenib/trametinib – no adjustment for renal; reduce dose if GFR <30 (consult BNF)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Immunotherapy + steroids** – combining with high-dose steroids (prednisolone >10mg/day) may reduce antitumour efficacy; only use for life-threatening irAEs.
  - **BRAF/MEK inhibitors** – starting in asymptomatic brain metastases without local treatment (radiosurgery) can cause intratumoural haemorrhage.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Immune-related colitis (diarrhoea ± blood) – check stool cultures, stop immunotherapy, IV methylprednisolone if grade ≥3
  - Immune-related pneumonitis – new cough/dyspnoea → high-res CT, hold immunotherapy
  - Anaphylaxis to immune checkpoint inhibitors (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Lymphoedema after SLNB or dissection (early physiotherapy)
  - Tumour recurrence – in-transit metastases, locoregional
  - Secondary cancers (risk higher with BRAF inhibitors – cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, keratoacanthoma)

### Prognosis
- **5-year survival:** Stage I ~98%; stage II ~85–95%; stage III ~40–80% (depending on nodal burden); stage IV ~20% (with immunotherapy, 5-year survival up to 50% in some trials). Prognosis heavily driven by Breslow thickness, ulceration, and mitotic rate.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always dermoscope any pigmented lesion** – even if you think it’s benign; it only takes 30 seconds.
- **Take a clinical photograph** before biopsy – essential for surgical planning and medicolegal record.
- **Check BRAF status on all stage III/IV melanomas** – opens targeted therapy options.
- **In immunotherapy patients, check a baseline cortisol and TSH before each cycle** – adrenal insufficiency can present insidiously.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **NEVER shave biopsy a suspicious pigmented lesion** – it destroys Breslow measurement.
- **Do not excise a melanoma with narrow margins (<1cm)** – recurrence risk rises.
- **Do not automatically start steroids for immunotherapy side effects without consulting oncology** – they may blunt therapeutic response.
- **Missing in-transit metastases** – they are palpable nodules <2cm from primary scar; excise with clear margin.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New or changing pigmented lesion elsewhere
  - Unexplained fever, rash, diarrhoea, or new cough (suspected irAE)
  - Swollen lymph nodes (especially axilla/groin)
  - Headache lasting >48 hours or any neurological symptom
- **Follow-up:**
  - Dermatology review in 2 weeks for excision results
  - Oncology referral if stage ≥IIB or high-risk features

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (SpR/Consultant) if:**
  - Suspicious lesion in a pregnant woman (melanoma can metastasise to placenta)
  - Rapidly growing melanotic lesion with satellite nodules (indicates aggressive disease)
  - Immunotherapy patient with any of: temperature >38°C, new confusion, hypotension, hypoxia, haemoptysis, or bloody diarrhoea >6 stools/day
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - GCS ≤12
  - Hypoxia (SpO₂ <90% on room air) – think pneumonitis
  - Septic shock (MAP <65 despite fluids)
